q: Stimulus is a detectable change in the internal or external environment.
Stimuli influence a permanent increase of physiological activity in the whole plant or any part of plant.
In the plant, there are three types of stimuli.
The responses to external stimuli that are produced by the working of various organs in a systematic manner are known as combination.
The movement of plant parts is due to various external and internal stimuli.
External stimuli of plant are water, gravity, light intensity, touch, chemicals and temperature.
Internal stimuli of plant are chemicals called plant hormones.
Animals lack a nervous system to respond rapidly to stimuli.
Coordination in the living organism is essential for the control of different activities.
Like animals, plants can move from one place to another to escape danger.
Like animals, plants also show coordination.
Plants respond to stimuli by changing their growth patterns.
Growth is defined as a permanent increase in size and dry mass of an organism.
Growth results from the division of zygote.
The change during growth is reversible.
Metabolic processes require a supply of energy.
Growth involves three phases: cell division, cell elongation and cell maturation.
Cells of meristematic region are with thick cell wall.
In cell elongation, cell wall is stretched out due to turgidity.
Cells in cell maturation phase are highly vacuolated.
In cell differentiation, cells undergo structural changes to perform general functions.
The optimum temperature for plant growth ranges between 20째C and 35째C.
Turgidity of cells is necessary for cell elongation.
Enzymatic activities decrease with the rise of temperature from 0째C to 35째C.
Respiration provides energy needed for the synthesis of protoplasm.
Water is required for the synthesis of protoplasm.
Development is the process of a series of changes through which an organism passes during its life cycle.
Structure and function are not closely related during development.
Growth and development of plants are controlled by external factors called phytohormones.
Phytohormones are organic substances.
Phytohormones are produced naturally in high concentrations.
Phytohormones regulate physiological processes of plants.
Phytohormones are specific in their action in a single species.
Phytohormones include growth promoters and growth inhibitors.
Growth promoters promote dormancy.
The group of plant growth regulators consists of auxins, gibberellins, cytokinins, ethylene and abscisic acid.
Auxin is a weak organic acid.
Auxins have an indole ring structure.
IAA is the largest and most important native auxin.
Auxins are produced in region of rapid cell division.
Auxins move towards the site of action by active transport.
In the root, high auxin concentration promotes growth.
Auxins inhibit the growth of lateral buds.
In stem, high auxin concentration stimulates growth.
Auxins cause the formation of unpollinated fruits.
Application of auxins prevents premature abscission of leaves and fruits.
Auxins promote the cell division in vascular cambium.
Auxins are used to initiate rooting in stem cuttings.
Parthenocarpy is the formation of seedless fruits without fertilization.
Synthetic auxins are used to accelerate fruit enlargement.
2,4-D is a selective weedicide.
Gibberellic acid (GA) was first isolated from a fungus.
Gibberellins are transported through phloem only by diffusion.
Gibberellins break the dormancy of seeds and buds.
Gibberellins increase the size of fruits.
Gibberellins promote stem elongation by cell division only.
Gibberellins stimulate the synthesis of hydrolytic enzymes during germination.
Cytokinins are basic in nature and purine derivatives.
Cytokinins delay senescence in plant organs.
Cytokinins promote the growth of lateral buds.
Cytokinins induce flowering in short-day plants.
Kinetin is a natural cytokinin.
Zeatin is a synthetic cytokinin.
Ethylene is a gaseous plant hormone.
Ethylene inhibits the ripening of fruits.
Ethylene increases horizontal growth of seedlings.
Ethylene accelerates abscission of leaves and flowers.
Ethylene breaks seed and bud dormancy.
Ethephon is a source of ethylene.
Abscisic acid (ABA) is a growth promoter.
ABA stimulates stomatal opening during water stress.
ABA acts as an antagonist to gibberellins.
ABA induces dormancy of buds and seeds.
ABA delays senescence of leaves.
During seed germination, water enters the seed through the micropyle.
The plumule emerges from the seed first during germination.
In epigeal germination, cotyledons remain below the ground.
In hypogeal germination, cotyledons are pushed above the ground.
Vivipary is the germination of seeds while still attached to the parent plant.
Photoperiodism is the response of plants to the relative lengths of day and night.
Plants can be classified into three groups based on photoperiodic responses.
Short-day plants flower when day length is longer than critical photoperiod.
Long-day plants flower when day length is shorter than critical photoperiod.
Day-neutral plants are independent of photoperiod for flowering.
The photoperiodic stimulus is perceived by the roots.
Florigen is a hypothetical flowering hormone.
Florigen moves from leaves to buds to induce flowering.
Photomorphogenesis is the growth and development of plants in response to light.
Light controls chlorophyll synthesis.
Seedlings grown in dark show normal development.
Phytochrome is a pigment involved in photomorphogenesis.
Phytochrome exists in two interconvertible forms.
Pr absorbs red light and changes into Pfr.
Pfr is the biologically active form of phytochrome.
Phytochrome is a protein conjugated with a chromophore.
Cryptochromes are blue-light photoreceptors.
Phototropins regulate phototropism.
In plants, coordination is entirely chemical.
In animals, coordination involves both nervous and endocrine systems.
Nervous messages are transmitted as electrical impulses.
Endocrine organs secrete chemical messengers called hormones.
Hormones are transported via the bloodstream.
Nervous coordination is slow but long-lasting.
Hormonal coordination is rapid and short-lived.
The human nervous system is divided into CNS and PNS.
The CNS includes the brain and spinal cord.
The PNS consists of cranial and spinal nerves.
The brain is protected by the skull and meninges.
The cerebrospinal fluid protects the CNS from mechanical shocks.
The human brain consists of forebrain, midbrain and hindbrain.
The cerebrum is the smallest part of the human brain.
The cerebral cortex is composed of grey matter.
The cerebrum is responsible for thinking, memory and language.
The hypothalamus regulates body temperature.
The midbrain controls reflex movements of eyes and ears.
The hindbrain includes the cerebellum, pons and medulla oblongata.
The cerebellum coordinates voluntary muscular movements.
The medulla oblongata controls involuntary vital activities.
The spinal cord functions as a conduction pathway.
Grey matter of spinal cord is located on the outside.
White matter consists of myelinated nerve fibres.
A reflex action is a rapid, automatic response to a stimulus.
The pathway of a reflex action is called a reflex arc.
A simple reflex arc involves at least three neurones.
Sensory neurones carry impulses from receptors to the CNS.
Motor neurones carry impulses from the CNS to effectors.
Interneurones are found inside the CNS.
The somatic nervous system controls involuntary actions.
The autonomic nervous system regulates involuntary actions.
The ANS consists of sympathetic and parasympathetic divisions.
The sympathetic system prepares the body for emergency situations.
The parasympathetic system promotes calming and normal functions.
Sympathetic stimulation increases heart rate.
Parasympathetic stimulation stimulates digestion.
Nerve cells are called neurones.
A neurone consists of a cell body, dendrites and an axon.
Dendrites conduct impulses away from the cell body.
The axon conducts impulses towards the cell body.
The myelin sheath acts as an electrical insulator.
Nodes of Ranvier are gaps in the myelin sheath.
Glial cells provide support and nutrients to neurones.
The junction between two neurones is called a synapse.
Transmission across a synapse is purely electrical.
Neurotransmitters are stored in synaptic vesicles.
Acetylcholine is a common neurotransmitter.
The synaptic cleft is the narrow gap between neurones.
Receptors for neurotransmitters are located on the presynaptic membrane.
Parkinson's disease is due to a deficiency of dopamine.
Alzheimer's disease is linked to a deficiency of acetylcholine.
Serotonin deficiency can lead to depression.
Endorphins act as natural painkillers.
